key_insight: A stepped semi-coherent Cr/Ni eutectic interface with nanoscale Ni segregation enables strain-gradient-driven dislocation activity, twinning, and interfacial shear so the hard BCC phase can plastically co-deform with the FCC phase instead of cracking.
engineering_relevance: Designing ultrafine eutectic microstructures with controlled interlamellar spacing and interface chemistry could yield high-strength metals that retain usable ductility under loading.
method: Electron-beam powder bed fusion to create a lamellar Cr-rich BCC / Ni-rich FCC eutectic (~450 nm spacing)
Atomic-resolution STEM / HR-STEM to resolve interface structure, Kurdjumov-Sachs orientation, and local Ni enrichment
In situ SEM micro-compression and tension plus correlative TEM to track deformation mechanisms
evidence_signal: The abstract reports stable high flow stress with large plastic strains and no cracking in in situ tests, supporting interface-mediated co-deformation.
watch_item: The mechanism is demonstrated for one ultrafine eutectic alloy system, so broader applicability will depend on whether similar interface structures and chemistry can be reproducibly engineered in other materials.
paper_title: Strengthening and interface-mediated plastic co-deformation in an ultrafine Cr-Ni eutectic: A nanomechanical investigation
